achang.clothing_summary: Achang traditional clothing reflects their Dai-influenced culture with distinctive black and blue tones. Womens attire features tight-fitting collarless jackets with silver buttons and elaborately embroidered belts.
key_features: High-collared collarless jackets with silver dome buttons
Embroidered belts (bai jian dai) with intricate geometric patterns
Black turbans for married women, colorful headscarves for unmarried
Mens front-fastening short jackets with wide-leg trousers
Hemp-woven fabric as a traditional textile base
achang.main_garments: Women wear collarless, tight-fitting blue or black jackets fastened with silver buttons at the right side, paired with a long skirt or trousers and a heavily embroidered waist belt. Men wear blue or black front-fastening short jackets with cloth buttons and loose wide-leg trousers.
achang.headwear: Married women wrap their hair in black cloth turbans, while unmarried women wear colorful headscarves with embroidered edges. Men traditionally wear white or blue cloth head wraps.
achang.embroidery: Achang embroidery features geometric patterns including zigzags, diamonds, and stylized floral motifs, predominantly on belts, collar edges, and cuffs. Silver ornaments are integrated into embroidered surfaces.
achang.colors: Deep blue, black, and white dominate daily wear, with bright red, green, and pink used for festive embroidery accents and accessories.
achang.festival_attire: During the Aolu Festival and Spring Festival, women wear their finest silver-buttoned jackets with the most elaborate embroidered belts and full silver jewelry sets, including earrings and bracelets.
achang.modern_influence: Achang textile techniques, particularly their hemp weaving, are preserved in craft cooperatives in Yunnan. Some contemporary Chinese fashion designers incorporate Achang silver button motifs into modern clothing.
achang.blockquote: The Achang peoples mastery of metalwork is so revered that their ancient knife-making tradition is said to have influenced the distinctive silver ornaments adorning their ceremonial garments.
achang.image_keywords: Achang ethnic group Yunnan traditional costume silver buttons embroidered belt
achang.fun_fact: The Achang are famous for their Huludao (gourd) knife-making tradition, and their silver buttons are often crafted by local silversmiths using techniques passed down through generations.

bai.clothing_summary: Bai clothing is renowned for its pristine white fabric as the primary color, symbolizing purity and virtue. The attire is richly accessorized with intricate embroidery, silver ornaments, and a distinctive phoenix-shaped headdress for women.
key_features: White as the dominant base color symbolizing purity
Phoenix-shaped headdress (fengguan) for women with floral embroidery
Silver butterfly-shaped clasps on capes and collars
Embroidered apron with colorful floral patterns
Seven-streamer waistband (qiyaodai) with intricate tassels
bai.main_garments: Women wear a white tunic as the innermost layer, covered by a pink, blue, or purple sleeveless jacket, secured with a silver chain. An embroidered apron is tied at the waist with colorful tasseled streamers. Men wear white shirts with black or blue front-fastening jackets, loose trousers, and a cloth belt.
bai.headwear: Unmarried Bai women wear a distinctive headpiece featuring a crown-like arrangement with upward-curving ends resembling a phoenix, decorated with floral embroidery and silver ornaments. Married women wear simpler embroidered headscarves. Men wear white or blue cloth head wraps.
bai.embroidery: Bai embroidery is exceptionally refined, featuring butterflies, flowers, and auspicious birds on aprons, collars, cuffs, and headwear. Satin stitch and split stitch techniques create three-dimensional floral patterns with vibrant color gradients.
bai.colors: White dominates as the primary color, with vivid accents of red, green, blue, pink, and purple on embroidered elements. Silver provides metallic contrast as ornamentation.
bai.festival_attire: During the Third Month Fair (Sanyuejie) and the Raosanling Festival, women wear their most elaborate phoenix headdresses, multi-layered embroidered aprons, and complete silver jewelry sets including necklaces, earrings, and bracelets.
bai.modern_influence: The distinctive white-and-embroidered aesthetic of Bai clothing has become a cultural symbol of Dali tourism, with simplified versions worn by performers and available as souvenirs.
bai.blockquote: Scholars note that the Bai peoples reverence for white extends beyond clothing into their architecture, where whitewashed walls serve as canvases for exquisite ink paintings of landscapes and calligraphy.
bai.image_keywords: Bai ethnic group Dali traditional costume phoenix headdress white embroidered apron silver jewelry
bai.fun_fact: The custom of wearing white among the Bai people is so deeply rooted that their name in Chinese literally means White People, and the color symbolizes their ideal of moral purity and honesty.

blang.clothing_summary: Blang traditional attire is characterized by dark indigo-dyed fabrics with striking silver ornamentation. Women favor cylindrical skirts with embroidered hems and collarless jackets adorned with silver studs and beads.
key_features: Dark indigo-dyed fabric as the primary textile
Cylindrical ankle-length skirts with patterned hem bands
Silver studs and beads sewn onto jacket collars and front panels
Colorful beaded necklaces and earrings in geometric arrangements
Mens front-fastening jackets with decorative silver buttons
blang.main_garments: Women wear collarless, tight-fitting short jackets in dark blue or black, fastened with silver buttons, paired with a long cylindrical skirt reaching the ankles with embroidered bands at the hem. Men wear collarless front-fastening jackets with wide-leg trousers and a cloth belt.
blang.headwear: Women wrap their hair in black or dark blue cloth turbans, sometimes with floral decorations inserted at the temples. Some Blang women shave their eyebrows as a traditional beauty standard.
blang.embroidery: Blang embroidery is concentrated on skirt hems and jacket edges, featuring geometric diamond and zigzag patterns in red, yellow, and white threads against dark fabric. Beadwork is more prominent than thread embroidery.
blang.colors: Dark indigo blue and black form the base, with bright red, yellow, white, and green used for embroidery detailing and bead trim.
blang.festival_attire: During the Water Splashing Festival and the Gannian Festival, women wear their most heavily silver-ornamented jackets, multiple strands of bead necklaces, and full-length embroidered skirts.
blang.modern_influence: Blang indigo dyeing techniques are maintained by village cooperatives in Yunnan.
blang.blockquote: The Blang, one of Chinas oldest tea-growing peoples, often incorporate tea-leaf motifs into their embroidery patterns as a quiet homage to their ancestral livelihood.
blang.image_keywords: Blang ethnic group Xishuangbanna traditional costume indigo jacket silver ornaments beaded necklace
blang.fun_fact: The Blang people have a unique tradition where girls in some communities traditionally shave their eyebrows, considering it a mark of beauty.

bonan.region: Gansu Province (Jishishan Bonan, Dongxiang, and Salar Autonomous County)
bonan.clothing_summary: Bonan attire blends Islamic modesty with Central Asian and Chinese influences, featuring long robes for men and brightly colored headscarves for women. Embroidered floral patterns on collars, cuffs, and chest panels are a hallmark.
key_features: Long single-breasted robes for men with embroidered chest panels
Womens brightly colored headscarves in green, pink, and white
Floral embroidery on collar edges, cuffs, and chest panels
Silver and jade buttons on mens formal overcoats
Leather boots for men as part of full ceremonial dress
bonan.main_garments: Men wear long robes or short jackets with cloth buttons over white shirts, with a distinctive embroidered chest panel, paired with loose trousers and leather boots. Women wear collarless jackets with long trousers under a long robe or tunic, always with a headscarf.
bonan.headwear: Women wear headscarves in different colors by age: green or pink for young women, white for older women. Men wear white or black skullcaps.
bonan.embroidery: Bonan embroidery features delicate floral patterns on collars, cuffs, chest panels, and headscarf edges. Peonies, plum blossoms, and geometric borders are common motifs.
bonan.colors: Dark blues, blacks, and grays for base garments, with bright green, pink, red, yellow, and white in embroidery and headscarves.
bonan.festival_attire: During Eid al-Fitr and Eid al-Adha, men wear new long robes with the finest embroidered chest panels, while women wear their most beautiful headscarves and fully embroidered tunics.
bonan.modern_influence: Bonan embroidery is recognized as an intangible cultural heritage practice in Gansu.
bonan.blockquote: Like their Dongxiang neighbors, the Bonan people maintain that the most intricate embroidery on a womans headscarf edge is a silent testament to her patience and domestic virtue.
bonan.image_keywords: Bonan ethnic group Gansu traditional costume embroidered robe headscarf skullcap floral embroidery
bonan.fun_fact: The Bonan people are famous for their handmade waist knives (baoan yao dao), which are renowned across northwestern China and traditionally worn as part of formal attire.

buyei.region: Guizhou Province (Qiannan and Qianxinan Prefectures), Yunnan, Sichuan
buyei.clothing_summary: Buyei clothing is distinguished by exquisite batik dyeing techniques and finely detailed indigo textiles. Women wear beautifully pleated skirts with batik patterns and intricately embroidered jackets.
key_features: Masterful batik (laran) wax-resist dyeing on indigo fabric
Pleated batik skirts with concentric spiral and floral patterns
Silver-studded embroidered aprons tied at the waist
Right-opening jackets with embroidered collar and cuff bands
Handwoven cotton fabric as the foundation of all garments
buyei.main_garments: Women wear long-sleeved, right-opening jackets in indigo blue or black, with embroidered trim at collars and cuffs, over pleated batik skirts reaching below the knee. A heavily embroidered apron with silver studs covers the front. Men wear blue or black front-fastening short jackets with wide-leg trousers.
buyei.headwear: Women wrap their hair in black cloth or embroidered headbands. Unmarried women often wear a distinctive headscarf with batik patterns and embroidered ends.
buyei.embroidery: Buyei embroidery features floral and butterfly motifs in bright colors, typically executed on aprons, belt ends, and headwear, often combined with silver ornaments.
buyei.colors: Indigo blue is the foundational color, with batik patterns in white and light blue. Embroidery adds vibrant red, yellow, green, pink, and purple accents.
buyei.festival_attire: During the Sixth Month Festival and Spring Festival, women wear their finest deeply dyed indigo batik skirts, most elaborately embroidered jackets, and full sets of silver jewelry.
buyei.modern_influence: Buyei batik has gained recognition as an intangible cultural heritage, with textiles increasingly featured in interior design and ethnic-inspired fashion.
buyei.blockquote: Ethnographers consider Buyei batik among the most technically refined in China, with some patterns requiring up to seven separate wax-resist applications before the final indigo immersion.
buyei.image_keywords: Buyei ethnic group Guizhou batik pleated skirt indigo traditional costume silver embroidery
buyei.fun_fact: The Buyei have a saying that a woman without batik skills cannot find a husband - traditionally, mastery of wax-resist dyeing was considered an essential domestic art for every Buyei woman.

dai.clothing_summary: Dai traditional clothing is renowned for its graceful, figure-hugging silhouettes and vibrant peacock-inspired aesthetics. Women wear form-fitting skirts and blouses in jewel tones, while men favor collarless jackets and sarong-style trousers.
key_features: Figure-hugging tubular skirts with narrow hem for walking
Short tight-fitting blouses with puffed sleeves in jewel tones
Silver waist belts with ornamental buckles in floral designs
Peacock-inspired color palette reflecting Buddhist aesthetics
Mens collarless jackets paired with sarong-like trousers
dai.main_garments: Women wear a short, tight-fitting blouse with round collar and fitted sleeves, paired with a long tubular skirt that wraps around the body and falls to the ankle, cinched with a silver belt. Men wear a collarless short jacket with cloth buttons, paired with loose sarong-style trousers.
dai.headwear: Women in Dehong wear a distinctive tall turban-style headdress. In Xishuangbanna, women wear flowers tucked into their hair buns. Men wear cloth head wraps.
dai.embroidery: Dai embroidery features peacock motifs, floral patterns, and Buddhist-inspired designs, often in metallic gold and silver threads on ceremonial garments.
dai.colors: Jewel tones dominate: peacock blue, jade green, golden yellow, magenta, and vermilion. Daily wear features cooler blue, black, and brown. White is worn for religious occasions.
dai.festival_attire: During the Water Splashing Festival, women wear their finest peacock-colored silk skirts and blouses with full silver ornament sets.
dai.modern_influence: The Dai silhouette has strongly influenced fashion in Yunnans tourism industry, with adapted Dai-style skirts and blouses marketed as resort wear.
dai.blockquote: The Dai peoples veneration of the peacock manifests in their clothing as a living art form, with each garments color gradient and flowing silhouette echoing the birds iridescent plumage.
dai.image_keywords: Dai ethnic group Xishuangbanna traditional costume tubular skirt silver waist belt peacock design
dai.fun_fact: The Dai Water Splashing Festival is celebrated by throwing water - getting completely soaked in new clothes is considered auspicious, as water washes away bad luck from the previous year.

daur.clothing_summary: Daur traditional clothing reflects their nomadic heritage in Northeast China, with robes cut for horseback riding and layered construction for harsh winters. Fur-trimmed garments and distinctive blue cotton robes are characteristic.
key_features: Long blue cotton robes with side slits for horseback riding
Fur-trimmed collars and cuffs for winter warmth
Decorative cloth belts with hanging pouches and accessories
Womens embroidered insoles and boot tops with floral patterns
Separate sleeve covers as a distinctive protective garment
daur.main_garments: Men wear a long front-fastening robe reaching below the knees, in blue or gray, with a cloth belt tied at the waist, paired with wide-leg trousers and leather boots. Women wear similarly styled robes but with more embroidery at collars, cuffs, and hems.
daur.headwear: Men wear fur hats with ear flaps in winter and straw hats in summer. Women wrap their hair in black cloth or wear headscarves.
daur.embroidery: Daur embroidery features floral patterns including peonies, lotuses, and chrysanthemums on boot tops, insoles, pouches, and sleeve edges.
daur.colors: Blue is the predominant color, with gray, black, and brown for daily wear. Embroidery accents use red, pink, yellow, green, and white.
daur.festival_attire: During the Spring Festival and Daur traditional New Year, new blue robes with embroidered belts, fur-trimmed jackets, and embroidered boots are worn.
daur.modern_influence: Daur embroidery crafts are preserved as cultural heritage products in the Morin Dawa region.
daur.blockquote: The Daur robes distinctive side slits, which scholars trace to Khitan origins, reveal a design philosophy where practical equestrian mobility dictated every tailoring decision.
daur.image_keywords: Daur ethnic group Inner Mongolia traditional robe fur hat embroidered boots blue dege costume
daur.fun_fact: Unlike many other ethnic groups, the Daur have a tradition of wearing separate decorative sleeve covers that can be changed independently from the main garment.

deang.clothing_summary: Deang traditional attire is distinguished by its bold use of black, red, and white stripes woven into womens skirts, and the iconic large silver earrings worn by women.
key_features: Horizontal red, black, and white striped tubular skirts
Large cylindrical silver earrings as a signature accessory
Multiple lacquered or silver waist hoops as a beauty marker
Collarless short jackets in dark blue with silver ornaments
Womens colorful beaded necklaces worn in multiple strands
deang.main_garments: Women wear a collarless, tight-fitting short jacket in black or blue, fastened with silver beads, paired with a distinctive striped tubular skirt. Multiple waist hoops of rattan lacquered in red or silver are worn daily. Men wear collarless front-fastening jackets with wide trousers.
deang.headwear: Women wrap their hair in black or dark blue cloth turbans adorned with small flowers or beads. Men wear red or black cloth head wraps.
deang.embroidery: Deang embroidery is minimal with more emphasis on woven stripe patterns and silver ornamentation rather than thread embroidery.
deang.colors: Black and dark indigo dominate, with vivid red, white, and green stripes on skirts providing bold contrast.
deang.festival_attire: During the Water Splashing Festival, women wear their full set of waist hoops, largest silver earrings, and most colorful striped skirts.
deang.modern_influence: The distinctive Deang striped skirt occasionally inspires contemporary textile designers exploring bold stripe patterns.
deang.blockquote: Ethnologists observe that the Deang womans waist hoops are not merely decorative - the number and material of hoops historically indicated her familys wealth and social standing.
deang.image_keywords: Deang Benglong ethnic group Yunnan traditional costume striped skirt silver earrings waist hoops
deang.fun_fact: Deang women traditionally believe the waist hoops originated when the first Deang woman wore rattan rings to protect herself from being captured by an evil spirit.

dong.clothing_summary: Dong clothing is famous for its exceptionally fine indigo-dyed fabrics that achieve a glossy metallic sheen through repeated dyeing and beating. Women wear exquisitely embroidered jackets and pleated skirts with elaborate silver ornaments.
key_features: Glossy indigo fabric achieved through repeated dyeing and stone-beating
Heavily pleated ankle-length skirts with embroidered waistbands
Elaborate floral embroidery on jacket fronts and cuffs
Massive silver headdresses with cascading ornaments
Hundreds of silver ornaments including neck rings and breastplates
dong.main_garments: Women wear collarless or right-opening jackets in glossy indigo black with embroidered trim, over a white inner blouse and a heavily pleated skirt. An embroidered apron covers the front. Men wear front-fastening short jackets in glossy indigo fabric with wide-leg trousers.
dong.headwear: Unmarried women wear elaborate silver crowns during festivals with dangling silver ornaments. Married women wrap their hair in cloth turbans.
dong.embroidery: Dong embroidery is exceptionally fine, featuring dragons, phoenixes, flowers, fish, and geometric patterns in colorful silk thread on dark fabric.
dong.colors: Glossy indigo black and deep blue dominate, with contrasting white inner collars. Embroidery introduces bright red, yellow, green, pink, and purple.
dong.festival_attire: During the Dong New Year and Grand Song Festival, women wear complete silver ornament sets: phoenix crown, neck rings, breastplate, earrings, and bracelets.
dong.modern_influence: Dong indigo-dyeing techniques and silver-crafting skills are recognized as national intangible cultural heritage.
dong.blockquote: The Dong peoples drum-song culture is matched by their visual artistry - a single festival garment may carry over two hundred individual silver pieces, each hand-crafted by village silversmiths.
dong.image_keywords: Dong ethnic group Guizhou traditional costume indigo pleated skirt silver headdress embroidery
dong.fun_fact: Dong fabric undergoes repeated indigo dyeing followed by beating with a stone roller and coating with egg white - this creates a distinctive blue-black sheen resembling polished leather.

dongxiang.region: Gansu Province (Linxia Hui Autonomous Prefecture, Dongxiang County)
dongxiang.clothing_summary: Dongxiang attire is characterized by Islamic modesty with Central Asian influences, featuring long robes, embroidered skullcaps, and womens colorful headscarves.
key_features: Mens white or black embroidered skullcaps
Womens colored headscarves varying by age (green, black, white)
Long front-fastening robes with dark tones and embroidered trim
Embroidered chest panels on mens formal jackets
Delicate floral embroidery on collar edges, cuffs, and shoe tops
dongxiang.main_garments: Men wear a white shirt under a short dark jacket or longer robe, with loose trousers and leather shoes. Women wear long, loose jackets with wide trousers beneath, always with a headscarf.
dongxiang.headwear: Womens headscarves are color-coded by life stage: green for young women, black for middle-aged, white for elderly. Men wear embroidered skullcaps.
dongxiang.embroidery: Dongxiang embroidery is concentrated on skullcaps, collar edges, cuffs, and shoe uppers, featuring small floral motifs like plum blossoms and peonies.
dongxiang.colors: Black, white, dark blue, and gray for base garments. Headscarves use green, blue, black, and white. Embroidery in bright red, pink, yellow, green.
dongxiang.festival_attire: During Eid al-Fitr and Eid al-Adha, new white shirts and robes are worn with the finest embroidery and best headscarves.
dongxiang.modern_influence: Dongxiang embroidery is preserved through local cultural industries in Linxia.
dongxiang.blockquote: Among the Dongxiang, the embroidery on a mans skullcap is considered a reflection of the women in his family - the finer the stitches, the greater the honor to his household.
dongxiang.image_keywords: Dongxiang ethnic group Gansu traditional costume embroidered skullcap headscarf long robe
dongxiang.fun_fact: The Dongxiang are one of the few ethnic groups in China whose name derives from their place of residence rather than from their own endonym.

evenki.clothing_summary: Evenki traditional clothing is adapted for life in the frigid forests of northeast China, with reindeer-hide garments and distinctive conical hats. Rich beadwork and metallic ornaments reflect shamanistic and nomadic aesthetics.
key_features: Reindeer-hide robes with fur trim for extreme cold protection
Distinctive conical hats with fur brims and beadwork
Extensive bead embroidery on chest panels and boot tops
Colorful applique patterns in geometric designs
Separate chest apron panels heavily decorated with silver and beads
evenki.main_garments: Both men and women wear long robes of reindeer or sheep hide, fastened with a leather belt, with a decorated chest apron worn over the robe. Fur-lined boots reach mid-calf.
evenki.headwear: The iconic Evenki hat is made from reindeer or fox fur, conical in shape with a rounded top, decorated with colored beadwork and metal ornaments.
evenki.embroidery: Evenki beadwork is exceptional - tiny colored seed beads sewn into geometric patterns, floral motifs, and stylized animal forms on chest panels, boot tops, and hat bands.
evenki.colors: Leathers natural brown and tan tones dominate. Beadwork introduces bright blue, red, yellow, green, and white.
evenki.festival_attire: During the Saurun Festival, Evenki wear their most elaborately beaded chest panels, finest fur robes, and full bead jewelry sets.
evenki.modern_influence: Evenki reindeer-hide craft and beadwork are maintained as cultural industries in the Aoluguya Evenki township.
evenki.blockquote: The conical Evenki hat is said to mimic the sacred reindeer antlers that connect the earthly and spiritual realms in Evenki shamanic cosmology.
evenki.image_keywords: Evenki ethnic group Inner Mongolia reindeer hide costume fur hat bead embroidery conical hat
evenki.fun_fact: The Evenki are one of the last reindeer-herding peoples in China, and every part of a reindeer is used in making their clothing.

gaoshan.region: Taiwan (indigenous groups: Amis, Atayal, Paiwan, Bunun, etc.)
gaoshan.clothing_summary: Gaoshan clothing varies among Taiwans diverse indigenous groups but is unified by the use of bark cloth, diamond-shaped embroidery, and elaborate beadwork. Shell bead embroidery and woven geometric patterns are distinctive hallmarks.
key_features: Diamond-shaped geometric patterns in woven and embroidered designs
Bark cloth made from beaten mulberry or paper mulberry bark
Thousands of tiny shell beads sewn into intricate patterns
Feathered headdresses and adornments for ceremonial use
Dense multicolored beadwork on ceremonial jackets and aprons
gaoshan.main_garments: Amis women wear black or blue short jackets with embroidered sleeves and colorful wraparound skirts. Atayal and Paiwan are known for their long shell-beaded tunics. Men traditionally wear loincloths and jackets of bark cloth or woven hemp.
gaoshan.headwear: Feathered headdresses are iconic - men wear caps or bands adorned with eagle, pheasant, or kingfisher feathers during ceremonies. Women wear floral garlands or embroidered headbands.
gaoshan.embroidery: Gaoshan embroidery is renowned for shell-bead work - tiny shell disks sewn onto dark fabric in geometric patterns including diamond, zigzag, and human-figure motifs.
gaoshan.colors: Black, dark blue, and natural browns for base fabrics. Beadwork uses vivid red, yellow, green, blue, white, and orange. Shell beads provide white contrast.
gaoshan.festival_attire: During harvest festivals and tribal ceremonies, the most elaborate bead-embroidered tunics and feathered headdresses are worn.
gaoshan.modern_influence: Indigenous designers in Taiwan have revitalized traditional shell-bead and weaving techniques in contemporary fashion.
gaoshan.blockquote: Among the Atayal, the shell-bead embroidery on a ceremonial robe was believed to require a thousand individual shell disks, each representing a prayer for the wearers protection.
gaoshan.image_keywords: Gaoshan Taiwanese indigenous traditional costume shell bead embroidery feathered headdress
gaoshan.fun_fact: The term Gaoshan encompasses at least 16 distinct indigenous groups in Taiwan, each with its own unique clothing traditions.

gelao.region: Guizhou Province (Wuchuan Gelao and Miao Autonomous County, Daozhen County)
gelao.clothing_summary: Gelao traditional clothing is known for its distinctive handwoven indigo fabric and unique collarless designs. Womens jackets feature wide sleeves and decorative side slits with intricate geometric embroidery.
key_features: Collarless front-fastening jackets with wide sleeves and side slits
Handwoven indigo-dyed hemp and cotton fabric
Geometric embroidery on collar panels, cuffs, and apron edges
Large solid silver earrings as a distinctive ornament
Mens collarless short jackets with cloth buttons and wide-leg trousers
gelao.main_garments: Women wear collarless jackets in dark indigo or black, with wide sleeves and deep side slits, paired with loose trousers and a heavily embroidered apron. Men wear simple collarless jackets and wide-leg trousers tied at the waist.
gelao.headwear: Women wrap their hair in cloth turbans in indigo, black, or white depending on local tradition. Some Gelao groups are named by their historical headwrap colors.
gelao.embroidery: Gelao embroidery features geometric patterns - diamonds, zigzags, and stepped pyramids - in bright threads on dark fabric, concentrated on collar panels and apron bibs.
gelao.colors: Indigo blue and black are predominant. Embroidery uses bright red, yellow, white, and green.
gelao.festival_attire: During the Gelao New Year and Ox King Festival, the most elaborately embroidered jackets and aprons are worn.
gelao.modern_influence: Gelao indigo textile techniques are preserved through intangible cultural heritage programs in Wuchuan County.
gelao.blockquote: The Gelao are known as one of the oldest inhabitants of Guizhou, and their side-slit jacket design reflects an ancient ergonomic wisdom for working in terraced rice paddies.
gelao.image_keywords: Gelao ethnic group Guizhou traditional costume indigo jacket collarless geometric embroidery silver earrings
gelao.fun_fact: The Gelao were traditionally subdivided into groups named by clothing color - the Red Gelao, White Gelao, Green Gelao, and Blue Gelao.

gin.clothing_summary: Gin traditional attire closely resembles the Vietnamese ao dai, with elegant fitted tunics and flowing trousers in soft pastels. The Gin are culturally connected to the Kinh majority of Vietnam.
key_features: Fitted long tunic with side slits worn over trousers
Soft pastel colors: light blue, pink, white, cream
Standing collar with concealed front closure
Mens matching tunics in darker tones with simpler cuts
Palm-leaf conical hats as a complementary accessory
gin.main_garments: Women wear a close-fitting long tunic with a standing collar, split at the sides, worn over loose white or matching trousers. Men wear a simpler four-panel gown or short jacket with trousers.
gin.headwear: The conical palm-leaf hat is iconic for women working outdoors. Women also wear floral headbands or hair buns with decorative combs.
gin.embroidery: Gin garments feature delicate embroidery on the collar, front placket, and lower hem with small floral patterns in matching or contrasting thread.
gin.colors: Pastels dominate: soft pink, lavender, light blue, cream, pale yellow, and white. Darker blues and blacks for daily wear and mens clothing.
gin.festival_attire: During the Gin Ha Festival, women wear their finest silk ao dai-style tunics in bright pastels, and men wear long gowns.
gin.modern_influence: Gin clothing is nearly identical to the Vietnamese ao dai, a globally recognized garment.
gin.blockquote: As Chinas only maritime ethnic group, the Gin peoples clothing aesthetic mirrors the soft coastal palette of their island homes - pale blue like the sea at dawn, pink like the shells along their shores.
gin.image_keywords: Gin Jing ethnic group Guangxi traditional costume ao dai long tunic conical hat pastel silk
gin.fun_fact: The Gin are Chinas only ethnic group of Vietnamese origin, arriving on three small islands off the Guangxi coast over 400 years ago.

hani.region: Yunnan Province (Honghe, Jiangcheng, Mojiang, Puer regions)
hani.clothing_summary: Hani traditional clothing is defined by its deep indigo and black tones accented with striking silver ornaments, and the famous multi-layered embroidered collars worn by women.
key_features: Multi-layered overlapping embroidered collars forming a V-shape
Silver studs and cones densely covering jacket fronts
Short jackets paired with short pleated skirts
Leg bindings with beaded and embroidered decorations
Bamboo or silver caps adorned with beads and feathers
hani.main_garments: Women wear a short collarless jacket in indigo black with silver cones covering the front, over a short pleated skirt. The multi-layered embroidered collar is the most distinctive feature. Men wear front-fastening jackets with wide trousers and a headwrap.
hani.headwear: Unmarried women wear a distinctive cap with silver spikes and coins attached, resembling a crown of metallic rays. Married women wear larger caps decorated with beads and feathers.
hani.embroidery: Hani embroidery is intricate, especially on the multi-layered collars, cuffs, and leg bindings. Geometric patterns and small floral motifs in bright colors on dark indigo.
hani.colors: Deep indigo and black provide the base, with embroidered accents in bright red, yellow, green, white, and pink. Silver studs and coins create metallic contrast.
hani.festival_attire: During the Kuzhazha Festival and Niang Niang Festival, women wear full sets of layered collars, most silver-studded jackets, and finest headdresses.
hani.modern_influence: Hani silver-working and embroidery techniques are preserved in Honghe Prefectures cultural heritage programs.
hani.blockquote: Anthropologists note that the Hani womans multi-layered collar is both garment and biography - each embroidered layer traditionally commemorates a significant milestone in her life.
hani.image_keywords: Hani ethnic group Yunnan Honghe traditional costume indigo silver studs multi-layered collar pleated skirt
hani.fun_fact: Hani womens short pleated skirts are designed for freedom of movement while working in the Honghe Hani rice terraces, a UNESCO World Heritage site.

han.clothing_summary: Han Chinese traditional clothing, known as Hanfu, has evolved over thousands of years through successive dynasties, featuring cross-collared robes, broad sleeves, and flowing silhouettes. Key historical styles include the Shenyi, Tang Qixiong Ruqun, and Ming Aoqun.
key_features: Cross-collared right-over-left closure as a defining feature
Broad flowing sleeves for formal garments
Jade belt ornaments and pendants for rank decoration
Silk brocade with dragon, phoenix, and cloud motifs
Layered construction with inner shirt, middle robe, and outer jacket
han.main_garments: The Shenyi is a one-piece long robe wrapping around the body. The Ruqun pairs a short jacket with a long wrap skirt for women. The Ming Aoqun combines a jacket with long pleated skirts. Tang Qixiong Ruqun has a high-waisted skirt tied above the bust.
han.headwear: Men wore gauze hats for officials, hairpins for scholars, and various caps for commoners. Women wore elaborate hair buns with hairpins, combs, and jeweled coronets for brides.
han.embroidery: Han embroidery includes four major schools: Suzhou, Hunan, Sichuan, and Guangdong styles, featuring silk-thread depictions of dragons, phoenixes, peonies, and auspicious symbols.
han.colors: Yellow reserved for the emperor, red for weddings, blue and green for scholars, purple for officials. The five primary colors corresponded to the five elements.
han.festival_attire: During Spring Festival, new red clothing is worn. Brides wear a red embroidered jacket and skirt, grooms wear a red or dark blue long robe.
han.modern_influence: The Hanfu revival since the early 2000s has spawned a substantial industry with annual sales exceeding one billion yuan.
han.blockquote: The Hanfu revival of the 21st century represents what cultural historians call a reclamation of embodied heritage - where wearing a specific collar shape becomes an act of historical identity.
han.image_keywords: Hanfu traditional Chinese clothing cross collar long robe silk brocade Ming Tang dynasty aoqun ruqun
han.fun_fact: The Hanfu revival has spawned subcultures separated by dynasty - Tang-style enthusiasts, Song-style purists, and Ming-style devotees each argue for their preferred periods aesthetic.

hezhen.clothing_summary: Hezhen traditional clothing is uniquely crafted from fish skin, earning them the historical name Fish Skin Tribe. Their garments are decorated with dyed fish-skin applique and cloud-scroll patterns.
key_features: Fish-skin processed into wearable leather
Fish-skin trousers and boots waterproof for fishing
Dyed fish-skin applique in cloud scroll and geometric patterns
Dog-fur trim on winter parka hoods and cuffs
Hempen cloth robes for summer wear as alternative
hezhen.main_garments: Men and women traditionally wore robes from salmon or pike skin, with the scales removed and skin softened through repeated pounding. The fish-skin robe is knee-length with wide sleeves, decorated with dyed fish-skin applique at hem, collar, and cuffs.
hezhen.headwear: Winter hats were made from dog or fox fur with ear flaps. Summer hats were simple cloth caps or headbands.
hezhen.embroidery: Rather than thread embroidery, the Hezhen specialize in fish-skin applique - pieces of fish skin dyed with natural pigments cut into scroll patterns and sewn onto garments.
hezhen.colors: Natural fish-skin tones from cream to light brown. Dyed applique adds blue, red, yellow, brown, and black.
hezhen.festival_attire: During the Wurigong Festival, the most elaborately decorated fish-skin robes with full applique coverage are worn.
hezhen.modern_influence: Hezhen fish-skin clothing craftsmanship is recognized as a national intangible cultural heritage.
hezhen.blockquote: The Hezhen mastery of fish-skin garment-making represents one of the worlds most specialized textile technologies - a complete set of clothing requires skins from approximately fifty large salmon.
hezhen.image_keywords: Hezhen Nanai ethnic group Heilongjiang fish skin costume traditional applique fur hat
hezhen.fun_fact: The Hezhen are one of the smallest ethnic minorities in China and the only one traditionally known for making clothing from fish skin.

hui.region: All provinces, concentrated in Ningxia, Gansu, Qinghai, Xinjiang, Henan
hui.clothing_summary: Hui traditional attire reflects Islamic dress codes combined with Chinese and Central Asian influences. Men wear distinctive white or black embroidered skullcaps, while women wear color-coded headscarves.
key_features: Mens white embroidered skullcaps worn at all times
Womens headscarves color-coded by age: green, black, white
Long loose gowns and robes with modest silhouettes
Delicate embroidery on skullcaps, collar edges, and cuffs
Mens black or dark blue jackets worn over white shirts
hui.main_garments: Men wear a white shirt under a short dark jacket or longer robe with loose trousers. Women wear long-sleeved high-collared jackets or gowns with loose trousers, always with a headscarf.
hui.headwear: Womens headscarves indicate life stage: green for young women, black for middle-aged, white for elderly. Men wear white or black embroidered skullcaps.
hui.embroidery: Hui embroidery concentrates on skullcaps, collar edges, cuffs, and cloth shoes, with small floral and geometric patterns in bright threads.
hui.colors: White symbolizes purity and is the base color. Dark blue, black, and gray for outer garments. Headscarves in green, black, and white.
hui.festival_attire: During Eid al-Fitr and Eid al-Adha, new white shirts and finest embroidered skullcaps are worn.
hui.modern_influence: Hui clothing has influenced Islamic fashion in China, with many Muslim Chinese women combining traditional headscarves with modern clothing.
hui.blockquote: Among the Hui, a womans headscarf is more than religious modesty - its color silently announces her transition through life stages, a social grammar stitched in fabric.
hui.image_keywords: Hui ethnic group Chinese Muslim traditional costume white skullcap embroidered headscarf Ningxia
hui.fun_fact: The Hui are Chinas most widely distributed ethnic minority, found in every province and county nationwide.

jingpo.region: Yunnan Province (Dehong Jingpo and Dai Autonomous Prefecture)
jingpo.clothing_summary: Jingpo traditional clothing is distinguished by its bold use of black and red, the iconic silver-studded female jacket, and the distinctive red headwraps.
key_features: Black velvet front-fastening jackets with silver dome buttons
Sleeveless silver-studded womens jackets with extensive silver ornamentation
Colorful woven wool skirts with bold red, black, and white stripes
Red turbans for men and elaborately wrapped red headpieces for women
Long swords worn as part of mens formal attire
jingpo.main_garments: Women wear a black velvet sleeveless jacket with the front densely covered in silver hemispheres, over a white inner blouse, paired with a boldly striped woven skirt. Men wear a white or dark shirt under a short black jacket, with striped wraparound skirts or trousers, and always a red turban.
jingpo.headwear: Womens headwear varies: some wear a red cloth headwrap with beaded fringe. Married women wear a more subdued black turban. Mens red headwrap is a cone-shaped turban with one end pointing upward.
jingpo.embroidery: Jingpo women excel in weaving rather than embroidery. Their skirts feature complex woven geometric patterns including zigzags, diamonds, and stripes in multiple colors.
jingpo.colors: Black and red dominate as primary colors, with white for inner garments. Bold stripes in red, black, white, and green on skirts. Silver provides metallic accent.
jingpo.festival_attire: During the Munao Zongge Festival, women wear full silver-studded jackets and striped skirts, men in complete formal dress with red turbans and ceremonial long swords.
jingpo.modern_influence: Jingpo woven textiles are produced in Dehong cooperatives for both traditional garments and modern accessories.
jingpo.blockquote: The Jingpo silver-studded jacket, whose hem jingles with every step of the Munao dance, creates what ethnomusicologists describe as an accidental percussion line woven into the fabric of ceremony.
jingpo.image_keywords: Jingpo ethnic group Yunnan Dehong traditional costume silver studded jacket red turban striped skirt
jingpo.fun_fact: The Jingpo Munao Zongge Festival features a massive dance where thousands of people move in synchronized patterns - the silver ornaments on womens jackets create a collective jingling sound that becomes part of the music.

jino.clothing_summary: Jino traditional clothing is characterized by distinctive white conical caps, dark indigo fabric, and striking geometric embroidery in bold colors.
key_features: Distinctive white peaked conical caps worn by both genders
Dark indigo sleeveless jackets with color-blocked front panels
Bold geometric embroidery in red, yellow, blue on chest and back
Womens short pleated skirts with embroidered hem bands
Mens embroidered front-fastening jackets with decorative back panels
jino.main_garments: Women wear a dark indigo sleeveless jacket with heavily embroidered front, over a dark inner blouse, paired with a short pleated skirt. Men wear a dark jacket with cloth buttons, embroidered front panels, and loose trousers.
jino.headwear: The iconic Jino headpiece is a white peaked conical cap decorated with horizontal bands of dark blue or black fabric, worn by both genders.
jino.embroidery: Jino embroidery is bold and angular, featuring diamonds, zigzags, and stepped pyramids in thick bright threads on jacket chest panels, armholes, and skirt hems.
jino.colors: Dark indigo, black, and natural white form the base. Embroidery uses intense red, yellow, blue, green, and white.
jino.festival_attire: During the Temaoke Festival (Jino New Year), the most elaborately embroidered jackets and caps are worn with full sets of bead necklaces.
jino.modern_influence: Jino textile techniques are maintained through intangible heritage programs in Xishuangbanna.
jino.blockquote: To the Jino people who call themselves descendants of the mothers womb, the peaked white cap symbolizes the sacred mountain from which their ancestors emerged at the dawn of creation.
jino.image_keywords: Jino ethnic group Xishuangbanna traditional costume white conical cap geometric embroidery indigo
jino.fun_fact: The Jino were the last ethnic group officially recognized by the Chinese government (in 1979), and their unique pointed white caps are a marker of their distinct identity.

kazakh.region: Xinjiang (Ili Kazakh Autonomous Prefecture, Altay, Tacheng)
kazakh.clothing_summary: Kazakh traditional clothing reflects their nomadic pastoral heritage, with practical leather and sheepskin garments lavishly decorated with embroidery. Womens wedding dresses feature headdresses topped with eagle-owl feathers.
key_features: Mens sheepskin overcoats with decorative embroidery
Womens tall bridal headdresses with silver coins, coral, and feathers
Fur-trimmed leather or felt hats with ear flaps for winter
Embroidered vests and jackets with vibrant floral patterns
Riding boots of hand-stitched leather with decorative stitching
kazakh.main_garments: Men wear a white shirt under a short embroidered vest, with a long velvet robe for formal occasions, leather trousers, and knee-high boots. Women wear long dresses with wide sleeves, covered by a fitted velvet vest heavily embroidered in floral patterns.
kazakh.headwear: Womens headwear indicates marital status: unmarried girls wear embroidered skullcaps with feathers, married women wear white headscarves. The bridal saukele is conical, 60-70 cm tall, covered in silver coins and topped with eagle-owl feathers.
kazakh.embroidery: Kazakh embroidery features floral scrolls, rams horn motifs, and bird-wing patterns in vibrant colors on vests, caps, and bags. Chain stitch and satin stitch on velvet produce a raised, luxurious effect.
kazakh.colors: Deep reds, burgundies, blues, and greens for velvet. Embroidery uses bright red, yellow, orange, green, and white against dark backgrounds.
kazakh.festival_attire: During Nauryz (Kazakh New Year), women in richly embroidered velvet vests over silk dresses, men in long velvet robes with fur-trimmed hats.
kazakh.modern_influence: Kazakh embroidered textiles are produced in Xinjiang for cultural tourism. The saukele headdress is a symbol of Kazakh culture in museum collections worldwide.
kazakh.blockquote: The Kazakh bridal headdress, rising nearly two-thirds of a meter tall and laden with ancestral silver, transforms the bride into what folklorists call a walking altar of lineage memory.
kazakh.image_keywords: Kazakh ethnic group Xinjiang traditional costume embroidered velvet vest saukele headdress leather boots
kazakh.fun_fact: The Kazakh bridal headdress (saukele) is so precious that it is traditionally handed down from mother to daughter - some family heirlooms have been worn by brides for over three generations.

korean.clothing_summary: Korean traditional clothing (hanbok) is characterized by clean, elegant lines and vibrant colors. Women wear the iconic jeogori jacket and chima skirt, while men wear jeogori with baji trousers, distinguished by graceful curves.
key_features: Womens short bolero-style jacket with a long ribbon bow
Full floor-length wrap-around skirt with voluminous silhouette
Mens loose-fitting trousers tied at the ankles
Outer coat for formal occasions with wide sleeves
Bright white as the base color with vivid jewel-toned outer layers
korean.main_garments: Women wear a short jeogori reaching just below the armpit, with a long decorative ribbon tied at the front, paired with a full-length chima skirt wrapped around the body. Men wear a longer jeogori with roomy baji trousers tied at the ankle, and a durumagi overcoat for formal occasions.
korean.headwear: Women traditionally wore a ceremonial coronet (jokduri) for weddings. Men wore a black horsehair hat (gat) for formal occasions with a wide brim and cylindrical crown.
korean.embroidery: Korean embroidery features peonies, lotus flowers, butterflies, and the ten symbols of longevity. Gold and silk threads on satin produce refined pieces for ceremonial garments.
korean.colors: White is fundamental, with outer garments in jewel tones: deep red, royal blue, emerald green, purple, and golden yellow.
korean.festival_attire: During Seollal and Chuseok, families wear fine hanbok in vibrant colors. Brides wear red chima with yellow jeogori, grooms wear blue formal ensembles.
korean.modern_influence: Hanbok has experienced a strong revival in global fashion, adapted into modern silhouettes while retaining the signature jeogori line and chima volume.
korean.blockquote: The Korean hanboks genius lies in its restraint - where other traditions embroider exuberantly, the hanbok achieves its impact through pure color saturation and the lyrical curve of the jeogoris sleeve.
korean.image_keywords: Korean Chaoxian ethnic group hanbok traditional costume jeogori chima durumagi gat hat
korean.fun_fact: The Korean jeogori has become progressively shorter over the centuries - modern womens jeogori barely covers the bust, while 500 years ago it reached the waist.

kyrgyz.clothing_summary: Kyrgyz traditional clothing is defined by the elaborate bridal headdress, silver ornaments, and beautifully decorated fur and felt garments suited to life in the Pamir mountains. The white felt kalpak hat for men is a national symbol.
key_features: Mens white felt kalpak hat with black velvet brim
Womens tall conical bridal headdress with cascading silver and coral
Heavy silver necklaces with incised geometric patterns
Embroidered womens vests in floral and rams horn motifs
Fur-trimmed winter coats made from sheep or fox skins
kyrgyz.main_garments: Women wear a long dress under a heavily embroidered velvet vest with silver buttons and a long robe for cold weather. Men wear a white shirt under a long velvet robe with embroidered edges and leather belt.
kyrgyz.headwear: The kalpak - a white felt hat with pointed crown and black velvet brim - is the most iconic Kyrgyz garment. The bridal shekuele is a tall conical headdress covered in silver coins and coral.
kyrgyz.embroidery: Kyrgyz embroidery features bold floral scrolls, rams horn motifs, and geometric patterns in chain stitch and satin stitch on velvet, felt, and leather.
kyrgyz.colors: Red, burgundy, and blue velvet for vests. White for felt kalpaks. Embroidery in bright red, yellow, green, blue, and white.
kyrgyz.festival_attire: During Nowruz, embroidered velvet robes, white kalpak hats, and complete silver jewelry are worn.
kyrgyz.modern_influence: The Kyrgyz kalpak hat is recognized as a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage item and remains a potent symbol of Kyrgyz identity.
kyrgyz.blockquote: The Kyrgyz kalpak, whose four-paneled white felt crown represents the four seasons and four cardinal directions, transforms a practical head covering into an atlas of nomadic cosmology.
kyrgyz.image_keywords: Kyrgyz ethnic group Xinjiang traditional costume white kalpak hat embroidered vest silver jewelry
kyrgyz.fun_fact: The Kyrgyz are the only ethnic group in China whose name is shared with a modern nation-state (Kyrgyzstan).

lahu.clothing_summary: Lahu traditional clothing is dominated by black fabric with striking geometric applique and silver ornamentation. Womens jackets feature a distinctive V-neck opening with extensive silver stud decoration.
key_features: Black fabric as the mandatory base color
Extensive silver hemispheres on womens jacket fronts and collars
Distinctive applique bands in red and white across jacket shoulders
Geometric woven patterns on shoulder bags and headwraps
Mens front-fastening black jackets with red and white piping
lahu.main_garments: Women wear a collarless black jacket with V-neck opening, silver-studded front, paired with a black long skirt or trousers. Men wear black front-fastening jackets with colored trim, and black wide-leg trousers.
lahu.headwear: Men wear black cloth headwraps, sometimes with decorative woven bands. Women also wear black headwraps, with some groups adding colorful woven bands.
lahu.embroidery: Lahu decoration emphasizes applique and woven geometric patterns. Red, white, and yellow fabric strips are applied to jacket shoulders and hems. Silver studs provide ornamental detailing.
lahu.colors: Black is virtually the only base color. Applique and trim use red, white, yellow, and blue. Silver provides metallic contrast.
lahu.festival_attire: During the Lahu New Year (Kuidong Festival), new black garments with the most extensive silver ornamentation are worn.
lahu.modern_influence: Lahu silver-working traditions are preserved in Lancang County.
lahu.blockquote: The Lahu peoples devotion to black - rejecting all other colors as everyday wear - is so absolute that anthropologists describe their aesthetic as monochrome magnificence, where texture and silver alone provide the visual narrative.
lahu.image_keywords: Lahu ethnic group Yunnan Lancang traditional costume black jacket silver studs red applique
lahu.fun_fact: The Lahu name means tiger-skin people - legend says their ancestors were born from tiger and human union, and their black clothing with red applique honors the tigers dark stripes.

li.region: Hainan Province (Baoting, Lingshui, Qiongzhong, Changjiang, Ledong)
li.clothing_summary: Li traditional clothing is famous for its exquisite brocade weaving, one of Chinas oldest textile traditions. Women wear distinctive tubular skirts with intricate geometric patterns and shell jewelry.
key_features: Finely woven Li brocade with complex geometric patterns
Womens short fitted jackets with colorful embroidered front panels
Tubular skirts with intricately patterned woven bands
Extensive shell, bead, and silver jewelry worn in multiple strands
Mens front-fastening jackets with fine fabric and minimal decoration
li.main_garments: Women wear a short collarless jacket tied with strings showing an embroidered chest panel, paired with a tubular skirt with horizontal woven pattern bands. Men wear collarless front-fastening jackets with loose trousers.
li.headwear: Women wear their hair in a bun secured with a bone or silver hairpin. Some subgroups wear bamboo wide-brimmed hats for sun protection.
li.embroidery: Li brocade uses a distinctive waist-loom technique for complex geometric patterns including human figures, ancestral symbols, and frogs. Double-face embroidery is a prized skill.
li.colors: Black and dark blue dominate base garments. Brocade uses vibrant red, yellow, green, white, and blue. Coral and shell add natural tones.
li.festival_attire: During the March Third Festival (Sanyuesan), women wear their finest Li brocade skirts with the most intricate woven patterns and full shell jewelry sets.
li.modern_influence: Li brocade is inscribed on Chinas national intangible cultural heritage list and has been exhibited internationally.
li.blockquote: Li brocade represents what textile historians call a library woven in thread - each geometric motif encodes ancestral knowledge about cosmology, marriage, and lineage predating written language in Hainan.
li.image_keywords: Li ethnic group Hainan traditional costume brocade tubular skirt geometric weaving shell jewelry
li.fun_fact: The Li were the first people to cultivate coconuts on Hainan Island over 3,000 years ago, and their skirts often feature a coconut tree motif woven into the brocade.

lisu.region: Yunnan Province (Nujiang Lisu Autonomous Prefecture), Sichuan
lisu.clothing_summary: Lisu traditional clothing is renowned for its vibrant colors, especially among the Flowery Lisu who wear boldly striped woven skirts and elaborately decorated jackets.
key_features: Brightly striped woven skirts in multicolor horizontal bands
Embroidered front-fastening jackets with colored trim and shell decorations
Coral and agate bead necklaces worn in overlapping multiple strands
Mens white or blue jackets with embroidered collar bands
Distinctive bamboo or rattan waistbands worn by some subgroups
lisu.main_garments: Women wear a long-sleeved collarless jacket with wide embroidered trim, over a long striped skirt woven with horizontal bands of red, blue, green, white, and black. Men wear white or dark short jackets with embroidered edges and loose trousers.
lisu.headwear: Women wrap their hair in colorful turbans decorated with bead tassels and seashells. Flowery Lisu prefer colorful headwraps. Men wear simple black or white headwraps.
lisu.embroidery: Lisu embroidery concentrates on jacket collars, cuffs, and headwrap edges, with geometric patterns in bright colors. Cross-stitch and applique are common.
lisu.colors: Bold multicolor stripes on skirts. Jackets in black, navy, or white. Embroidery uses bright red, yellow, green, blue, and pink.
lisu.festival_attire: During the Kuoshi Festival (Lisu New Year), women wear their most vibrantly striped skirts and largest sets of coral bead necklaces.
lisu.modern_influence: Flowery Lisu striped skirts are popular in Yunnans ethnic tourism trade.
lisu.blockquote: To the Lisu, the breadth and brightness of a womans striped skirt is an unspoken announcement - the wider the band, the more prosperous her family, the richer the colors, the more skilled her weaving.
lisu.image_keywords: Lisu ethnic group Yunnan traditional costume striped woven skirt beaded necklace Nujiang
lisu.fun_fact: The Lisu are divided into three subgroups by clothing color: the Black Lisu, White Lisu, and Flowery Lisu - the Flowery Lisu wear the most colorful attire.

manchu.clothing_summary: Manchu traditional clothing introduced the iconic qipao to Chinese fashion, along with distinctive horse-hoof cuffs. Their clothing system became the foundation of Qing dynasty court attire.
key_features: Riding-style robes with side slits for equestrian mobility
Distinctive horse-hoof cuffs covering the hands
Womens one-piece qipao evolving from Manchu changpao
Mens magua riding jacket worn over long robes
Platform shoes for elite women
manchu.main_garments: The foundation is the long robe (changpao) with right-side closure, horseshoe cuffs, and side slits. Men wear a shorter riding jacket (magua) over the changpao. Women wear a similarly cut changpao or the more fitted qipao with stand-up collar and side slits.
manchu.headwear: Men wore a conical cap with tassel. Elite women wore the two-headed hair ornament (liangbatou) - a large bifurcated headdress decorated with flowers, pearls, and tassels.
manchu.embroidery: Qing dynasty Manchu embroidery set the standard for Chinese imperial embroidery. Five-clawed dragons, phoenixes, and eight Buddhist treasures in silk and gold thread.
manchu.colors: Imperial yellow for the emperor. Bright blue, red, purple for officials by rank. Commoners wore gray, blue, brown. Womens festive wear in vibrant red, pink, green.
manchu.festival_attire: During Spring Festival, finest changpao or qipao with dragon, phoenix, or floral embroidery and matching hair ornaments.
manchu.modern_influence: The qipao evolved into one of the most iconic Chinese garments worldwide. Qing court dress established the visual language of traditional Chinese clothing recognized globally.
manchu.blockquote: The Manchu horse-hoof cuff represents a remarkable synthesis of form and function - it simultaneously protected hands while riding and served as a canvas for embroidery announcing the wearers rank with every gesture.
manchu.image_keywords: Manchu ethnic group traditional qipao changpao horse hoof cuff magua Qing dynasty platform shoes
manchu.fun_fact: The Manchu qipao was originally a loose straight-cut garment for both genders - it was not until the 1920s in Shanghai that it evolved into the form-fitting high-slit dress recognized today.

maonan.clothing_summary: Maonan traditional clothing is characterized by elegant simplicity with indigo-blue or black jackets, embroidered collars, and distinctive silver accessories. The Maonan are known for exceptional bamboo hat craftsmanship.
key_features: Dark indigo front-fastening jackets with embroidered collar panels
Distinctive patterned bamboo hats with intricate weave
Silver hairpins, earrings, and bracelets with filigree work
Embroidered cloth shoes with floral patterns on the toe
Mens blue or black jackets with cloth buttons and matching trousers
maonan.main_garments: Women wear a front-fastening jacket in indigo or black with embroidered trim, paired with wide-leg trousers and a patterned cloth belt. Men wear similar jackets with cloth buttons. The bamboo hat is worn by both genders.
maonan.headwear: The Maonan bamboo hat is their most famous accessory - finely woven from bamboo strips with decorative patterns. Women also wear silver hairpins.
maonan.embroidery: Maonan embroidery features floral and butterfly motifs on jacket collars, cuffs, and cloth shoes. Cross-stitch and satin stitch in bright colors on dark fabric.
maonan.colors: Indigo blue and black dominate. Embroidery in red, pink, green, yellow, and white. Natural tan of bamboo provides warm contrast.
maonan.festival_attire: During the Fenlong Festival, women wear new indigo jackets with finest embroidery and full silver jewelry.
maonan.modern_influence: Maonan bamboo hat weaving is recognized as intangible cultural heritage.
maonan.blockquote: The Maonan bamboo hat is a masterpiece of functional art - a single hat may contain hundreds of hand-split bamboo strands so fine they are barely visible, creating fabric more water-resistant than most textiles.
maonan.image_keywords: Maonan ethnic group Guangxi Huanjiang traditional costume indigo jacket bamboo hat silver hairpin
maonan.fun_fact: Maonan bamboo hats (dingnan) take several days to weave and are so tightly constructed that they can hold water.

miao.region: Guizhou, Hunan, Yunnan, Sichuan, Guangxi, Hubei
miao.clothing_summary: Miao traditional clothing is among the most visually spectacular of all Chinas ethnic groups, renowned for extraordinary silver jewelry and intensely embroidered garments. Festival attire can include hundreds of silver ornaments.
key_features: Massive silver headdresses with horn-shaped extensions
Intricate pleated skirts with batik, embroidery, and applique layers
Heavy silver neck rings in graduated sets covering the chest
Exquisite embroidery in cross-stitch, satin stitch, and couching
Distinctive subgroup styles: Long-horn Miao, Batik Miao, Flowery Miao
miao.main_garments: Women wear a collarless jacket in dark blue or black with dense embroidery on the opening, over a stunning pleated skirt combining batik, embroidered bands, and applique panels with up to 500 pleats. Men wear simpler dark jackets with embroidered bands and loose trousers.
miao.headwear: The silver crown-like headdress is spectacular - a large tiara with raised horn-like extensions, floral filigree, and dangling ornaments. Some subgroups wrap hair around oversized wooden or silver combs.
miao.embroidery: Miao embroidery is globally recognized as among the finest. Techniques include cross-stitch, satin stitch, chain stitch, and broken stitch. Motifs include dragons, phoenixes, butterflies, and geometric patterns.
miao.colors: Dark indigo, black, navy form the base. Embroidery in red, yellow, green, blue, white, pink, purple. Silver provides dominant metallic accent.
miao.festival_attire: During the Lusheng Festival and Miao New Year, women wear complete silver sets weighing up to 15 kilograms - headdress, neck rings, breastplate, earrings, bracelets.
miao.modern_influence: Miao embroidery and silver craftsmanship have been celebrated internationally. Dior and other designers have incorporated Miao techniques into haute couture.
miao.blockquote: A Miao woman in full festival silver is described by ethnographers as a walking treasury - her ornaments represent not only aesthetic mastery but the accumulated wealth of generations, worn publicly as both protection and pride.
miao.image_keywords: Miao Hmong ethnic group Guizhou traditional costume silver headdress pleated skirt embroidery batik
miao.fun_fact: A full Miao festival outfit can include 200-300 pieces of silver jewelry weighing up to 15 kilograms, making it one of the heaviest traditional costumes in the world.

monba.region: Tibet Autonomous Region (Cona County, Medog County)
monba.clothing_summary: Monpa traditional clothing shows strong Tibetan Buddhist influence with local adaptations, featuring long robes, colorful striped woven aprons, and ornately embroidered hats with feathers.
key_features: Long red or brown woolen robes with cloth belts
Distinctive woven hats with upturned brims and peacock-feather ornaments
Colorful striped woven aprons worn by women
Handwoven hemp and wool fabrics with striped patterns
Silver and turquoise earrings, necklaces, and amulet boxes
monba.main_garments: Women wear a long sleeveless robe in red or dark tones, fastened with a cloth belt, over a long-sleeved inner blouse. The striped apron is the most decorated garment. Men wear a longer robe with a cloth belt.
monba.headwear: The signature Monpa hat is made from woven fabric with an upturned brim, decorated with a peacock or pheasant feather. Women also wear headscarves or silver hair ornaments.
monba.embroidery: Monpa textile decoration relies more on woven patterns than embroidery. Their aprons feature woven horizontal stripes. Silk thread appears in small floral motifs on hats.
monba.colors: Red, brown, and maroon dominate (Tibetan Buddhist influence). Striped aprons add white, red, blue, green, yellow, and black.
monba.festival_attire: During Losar (Tibetan New Year), the finest red woolen robes, most elaborately striped aprons, and full turquoise and silver jewelry sets are worn.
monba.modern_influence: Monpa weaving techniques are maintained in Cona and Medog counties.
monba.blockquote: The Monpa hats upturned brim and single feather crest signal the wearers valley of origin in the high Himalayas where Monpa communities have lived for over a millennium.
monba.image_keywords: Monpa ethnic group Tibet traditional costume chuba striped apron woven hat pheasant feather
monba.fun_fact: The Monpa are the only ethnic group in China practicing both Tibetan Buddhism and indigenous Bon shamanism.

mongolian.region: Inner Mongolia, Xinjiang, Qinghai, Gansu, Heilongjiang
mongolian.clothing_summary: Mongolian traditional clothing (deel) is designed for the nomadic steppe lifestyle, featuring a distinctive long robe with diagonal front closure worn with a colorful sash. The attire is decorated with gold-thread embroidery and fur trims.
key_features: Long wrap-around robe with right-side diagonal closure
Brightly colored silk or wool sash tied at the waist
Gold-thread embroidery on collars, cuffs, and front panels
Distinctive pointed or rounded hats with fur or bead decorations
High leather riding boots with upturned toes and decorative stitching
mongolian.main_garments: The deel is a long one-piece robe reaching below the knee, with long sleeves and diagonal front closure, secured with a cloth belt tied tightly around the waist. An embroidered vest is worn over the deel for formal occasions.
mongolian.headwear: Men wear pointed fur hats in winter. Young women wear small caps with bead tassels. Married noble women wear the distinctive tall broad headdress adorned with silver, coral, turquoise, and peacock feathers.
mongolian.embroidery: Mongolian embroidery features floral scrolls, cloud patterns, and rams horn motifs in gold and silver thread. Embroidery appears on deel collars, cuffs, front placket, and boot uppers.
mongolian.colors: Blue (representing the eternal sky) is the most important. Red for fire, yellow for earth, green for pastures, white for purity. Gold and silver embroidery add brilliance.
mongolian.festival_attire: During the Naadam Festival, participants wear their finest deels. Wrestlers wear distinctive brief-like garments with decorated back panels and high boots.
mongolian.modern_influence: The Mongolian deel is worn daily by many in Inner Mongolia and has influenced Chinese and Central Asian fashion through its distinctive cut and decorative motifs.
mongolian.blockquote: The Mongolian deel is designed so that everything needed for steppe survival can be carried in its voluminous front - the robe is simultaneously garment, pocket, blanket, and shelter.
mongolian.image_keywords: Mongolian ethnic group Inner Mongolia traditional deel robe fur hat riding boots gold embroidery sash
mongolian.fun_fact: The Mongolian deel has no buttons - it is secured entirely by the sash (buse) wrapped around the waist, and the upper front panel forms a large pocket traditionally used to carry food, tobacco, and personal items.

mulao.region: Guangxi Zhuang Autonomous Region (Luocheng Mulao Autonomous County)
mulao.clothing_summary: Mulao traditional clothing features deep blue or black handwoven cotton with subtle embroidered accents. Women wear collarless jackets with distinctive silver buckles and wide-leg trousers with embroidered waistbands.
key_features: Deep blue collarless jackets with distinctive silver toggle buttons
Handwoven cotton fabric in dark indigo tones
Wide-leg trousers with embroidered waistband panels
Silver hairpins and earrings with simple elegant designs
Embroidered cloth shoes with upturned toes for women
mulao.main_garments: Women wear a collarless, front-fastening jacket in dark blue or black with silver toggle buttons and embroidered trim on the collar and cuffs, paired with wide-leg trousers reaching the ankles. An embroidered waistband peeks out below the jacket. Men wear front-fastening jackets in matching dark tones with cloth buttons and loose trousers.
mulao.headwear: Women traditionally wear their hair in a coiled bun secured with silver hairpins, sometimes covered with a simple cloth headband. Married women may wear a small embroidered cap or headwrap.
mulao.embroidery: Mulao embroidery is concentrated on waistbands, cuffs, shoe uppers, and the edges of headwear, featuring small geometric and floral motifs in bright threads on dark fabric.
mulao.colors: Dark blue and black dominate daily wear. Embroidery uses red, pink, green, yellow, and white. Silver provides subtle metallic accents.
mulao.festival_attire: During the Yifan Festival (Mulao Thanksgiving) and Spring Festival, women wear new dark blue jackets with the finest silver toggles and embroidered details.
mulao.modern_influence: Mulao indigo dyeing traditions are preserved through community workshops in Luocheng County.
mulao.blockquote: The Mulao people believe that the silver toggles on their jackets chime with each movement, creating a gentle music that wards off ill fortune during festival processions.
mulao.image_keywords: Mulao ethnic group Guangxi Luocheng traditional costume indigo jacket silver toggle buttons embroidered waistband
mulao.fun_fact: The Mulao celebrate the Yifan Festival every three years, a unique Thanksgiving-like tradition where entire villages dress in new clothes and offer thanks for the harvest.

naxi.clothing_summary: Naxi traditional clothing is one of the most recognizable in China due to the distinctive sheepskin cape with seven circular embroidered ornaments that women wear, representing the moon and stars. The cape, called xingyue bajian (Seven Stars Cape), is a cultural icon.
key_features: Seven Stars sheepskin cape with seven circular embroidered ornaments
Long wide-sleeved robe with a belt tied at the back
Embroidered collar and cuff bands in geometric Dongba-inspired patterns
Blue or purple gown with a cream or white front panel
Woven cloth headpiece with decorative beaded tassels for married women
naxi.main_garments: Women wear a full-length gown in blue, purple, or black, with a contrasting front panel, over a long skirt or trousers. Over the gown, they wear the iconic sheepskin cape - a whole sheepskin draped over the shoulders with seven embroidered circles resembling stars connected by decorative bands. Men wear front-fastening jackets over white shirts with loose trousers and a cloth belt.
naxi.headwear: Married women wear a distinctive headdress made of black or blue fabric shaped like a pagoda or coil, decorated with silver beads and red tassels at the sides. Unmarried women wear simpler headbands or weave colorful threads into their hair. Men wear white or blue head wraps.
naxi.embroidery: Naxi embroidery features patterns inspired by the ancient Dongba pictographic script - including stylized birds, flowers, clouds, and geometric symbols - on collars, cuffs, and the edges of the cape.
naxi.colors: Dark blue, purple, black, and brown for main garments. The sheepskin cape is natural tan and white. Embroidery uses bright red, yellow, green, and white. Silver and coral for jewelry.
naxi.festival_attire: During the Sanduo Festival (Naxi New Year) and the Torch Festival, women wear their finest seven-star capes with the most elaborate embroidery and full silver jewelry sets.
naxi.modern_influence: The Naxi seven-star cape has become a symbol of Lijiang and is frequently worn by performers and tourism workers. Naxi Dongba patterns appear in contemporary textile design worldwide.
naxi.blockquote: The seven embroidered stars on the Naxi cape are said to represent the Pleiades constellation, guiding the wearer through darkness, while the two larger discs symbolize the moon and sun watching over the people.
naxi.image_keywords: Naxi ethnic group Lijiang Yunnan traditional costume seven stars sheepskin cape Dongba embroidery
naxi.fun_fact: The Naxi are the only ethnic group in the world that still uses the Dongba pictographic script, the worlds last living hieroglyphic writing system - their embroidery often incorporates these ancient symbols.

nu.region: Yunnan Province (Nujiang Lisu Autonomous Prefecture, Gongshan County)
nu.clothing_summary: Nu traditional clothing is made from handwoven hemp and cotton fabric in natural tones, decorated with colorful geometric bands and beadwork. Women wear long skirts with distinctive striped hem bands.
key_features: Handwoven hemp and cotton fabric in natural brown and gray tones
Womens long skirts with bold horizontal striped hem bands
Colorful bead necklaces and bracelets in geometric arrangements
Collarless front-fastening jackets with piping trim
Men's cross-body shoulder bags with woven geometric patterns
nu.main_garments: Women wear a collarless jacket in dark blue or black with colored piping at the edges, paired with a long wraparound skirt featuring distinctive horizontal striped bands at the hem in red, blue, and white. Men wear front-fastening jackets in dark tones with loose trousers.
nu.headwear: Women wrap their hair in black or blue cloth, sometimes with bead-decorated headbands. Some Nu women favor woven headwraps with patterned ends hanging at the side. Men wear simple cloth head wraps.
nu.embroidery: Nu decoration focuses on woven geometric bands on skirt hems and shoulder bags rather than needle embroidery. Beadwork on necklaces and bracelets carries geometric patterns.
nu.colors: Dark blue, black, and natural hemp tones for daily wear. Skirt stripes in red, blue, white, and green. Beadwork in bright multicolors.
nu.festival_attire: During the Nu traditional New Year and the Mountain God Festival, the best striped skirts and full bead jewelry sets are worn.
nu.modern_influence: Nu hemp-weaving traditions are maintained through cultural preservation programs in the Nujiang region.
nu.blockquote: Living along the wild Nujiang River gorge, the Nu people incorporate the rivers energy into their garments - the bold horizontal stripes on their skirts echo the bands of color seen in the canyon walls at sunset.
nu.image_keywords: Nu ethnic group Yunnan Nujiang traditional costume hemp skirt striped hem beaded necklace woven bag
nu.fun_fact: The Nu people take their name from the Nujiang River (Salween River), one of the wildest and most remote rivers in the world, along whose gorges they have lived for centuries.

orogen.clothing_summary: Oroqen traditional clothing is crafted from animal hides, especially deer and roe deer, decorated with intricate geometric beadwork and fur edging. Traditional life as reindeer hunters shaped their distinctive peaked hoods and practical fur garments.
key_features: Deer-hide robes with fur trim and intricate beadwork
Distinctive peaked fur hoods with ear flaps
Geometric bead embroidery in diamond and triangle patterns
Fur-lined gloves and boots for extreme cold
Colorful applique bands on robe hems and cuffs
orogen.main_garments: Both men and women wear long deer-hide robes with the fur worn inward for warmth and the smooth hide outward, decorated with beadwork bands at the hem, cuffs, and front opening. Robes are fastened with a leather belt. Separate leggings and fur-lined boots complete the outfit.
orogen.headwear: The iconic Oroqen hat is a peaked cap made from deer or roe deer fur, sometimes with the animals ears still attached as decoration. Winter hats have fur ear flaps that can be tied up or down. Women wear beaded headbands under their fur hoods.
orogen.embroidery: Oroqen beadwork is exquisite, featuring geometric patterns including diamonds, triangles, zigzags, and stepped pyramids in colored seed beads on hide. Motifs often represent natural elements like mountains, rivers, and forests.
orogen.colors: Natural hide tones of brown and tan dominate. Beadwork introduces bright blue, red, yellow, green, and white. Fur trim in natural white, brown, and gray.
orogen.festival_attire: During the Oroqen Spring Festival celebrations and the Gathering Festival, the most elaborately beaded hide robes and fur hats are worn.
orogen.modern_influence: Oroqen hide-working and beadwork techniques are preserved as intangible cultural heritage in the Oroqen Autonomous Banner.
orogen.blockquote: The Oroqen practice of leaving the ears on fur caps is not simply decoration - it embodies the hunters belief that wearing the animals likeness honors its spirit and ensures successful future hunts.
orogen.image_keywords: Oroqen ethnic group Inner Mongolia traditional costume deer hide robe fur hat beadwork peaked hood
orogen.fun_fact: The name Oroqen means people who use reindeer or people on the mountain, and they were traditionally one of the only ethnic groups in China whose entire material culture was based on animal products - hide, fur, bone, and antler.

pumi.clothing_summary: Pumi traditional clothing is characterized by white sheepskin capes, long multicolored pleated skirts for women, and dark jackets with red trim for men. The white sheepskin cape is the most distinctive Pumi garment.
key_features: White sheepskin cape worn over the shoulders
Long multicolored pleated skirts with horizontal woven bands
Large silver earrings and coral bead necklaces
Dark front-fastening jackets with red or blue trim
Men's white linen or hemp trousers with embroidered leg bands
pumi.main_garments: Women wear a long pleated skirt in white or natural wool with colored horizontal bands, a dark blue or black jacket with embroidered collar and cuffs, and the iconic white sheepskin cape draped over the back and shoulders. Men wear a dark jacket with red piping over a white shirt, paired with wide-leg white trousers.
pumi.headwear: Women wrap their hair in a large black or dark blue cloth headdress that extends down the back, called a waba. Some styles incorporate colorful beaded bands and silver ornaments at the temples. Men wear black or blue head wraps.
pumi.embroidery: Pumi embroidery concentrates on jacket collars, cuffs, and the edges of the sheepskin cape, featuring floral and geometric patterns in bright threads. The cape edges often bear embroidered bands in red, yellow, and blue.
pumi.colors: White dominates (especially the sheepskin cape), with dark blue or black jackets. Skirt bands in red, blue, green, and white. Embroidery in bright primary colors.
pumi.festival_attire: During the Pumi New Year (Wugu Festival) and the Kuoshi Festival, women wear their finest white sheepskin capes, most elaborately pleated skirts, and full coral bead sets.
pumi.modern_influence: Pumi sheepskin cape craftsmanship is maintained as cultural heritage in Ninglang County.
pumi.blockquote: The Pumi white sheepskin cape, worn daily by women for centuries, is considered by cultural historians to be a living fossil of the ancient Qiang sheepherding tradition that dates back over 2,000 years.
pumi.image_keywords: Pumi ethnic group Yunnan traditional costume white sheepskin cape pleated skirt coral beads silver earrings
pumi.fun_fact: The Pumi believe that the white sheepskin cape protects not just from cold but from evil spirits - young Pumi women traditionally receive their first sheepskin cape as a protective talisman when they come of age.

qiang.region: Sichuan Province (Aba Tibetan and Qiang Autonomous Prefecture, Beichuan County)
